speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 18, 1995. The survivor is Edith Sheldon, her maiden name is Drucker. The interviewer is Cyndi Kaplan. This is Sydney, Australia. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello, I'm Cyndi Kaplan. And I'm going to be interviewing Edith Sheldon in Sydney. And today is the 18th of September, 1995. Hello, Edith. Edith, could you give us your name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Edith Sheldon. S-H-E-L-D-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me on what date you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 7th of January, 1927, in Prague, Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-R-A-G-U-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And Edith, can you tell me what your maiden name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-R-U-C-K-E-R, Drucker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what I'd like to do is let's start with your very earliest memories, in your family. First of all, you can tell me a little bit about your parents, and then the town you were in and what life was like-- a whole picture of what life was like in your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. My mother came from near Karlsbad in northern Bohemia. Her family had been living there for over 300 years, in the same little place. They had to have the castle permission to be allowed to trade and to live there. My father came also from northern Bohemia, in-- he was born in Lovosice. And the family then moved to Theresienstadt, which is rather funny. [LAUGHS] And the family lived there till the deportations. My mother used to go for holidays to Theresienstadt, because her two aunts lived there. And she used to spend the school holidays there, where she made a friend with a girl-- became friendly with a girl who had an older brother. And that older brother eventually became my father. It was a childhood romance. And they courted, I think, for 10 years. The war intervened, World War I, of course. And then my father had to finish his engineering studies. And finally he had to get a job. Because those days, you didn't get married till you had a safe future. We lived in Prague, which is the capital or was the capital of Czechoslovakia. But my father died when I was two. He contracted middle ear infection. And being very conscientious, he continued to go to work, because he was building the first reinforced concrete building in Prague, a big palace-- a business house, actually-- a, a big house on the main square. And he was very concerned, because the winter was particularly cruel. And he was concerned that the mixing should go on properly. And he disregarded his temperature and later his fever. And unfortunately, he died very quickly. The medical profession those days didn't have antibiotics or anything like that. There was only a trepanation, which was performed too late. And it's already gone to his brain. And he died of a middle ear infection--

speaker: interviewer
text: Shame.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was--

speaker: interviewee
text: At the age of 35.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tragic.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was 31 then. And I was two. My mother never remarried. My mother was a music teacher by profession. [SIGHS] And she started first going to people's-- to families to teach the children. Later, when she became better known, she gave lessons in our home. And then she taught also German, because that was her mother language. And lots of Czech children, we had Czech-- German at school, in high school, compulsory. And a lot of Czechs liked to have their children taught German, because it was more a second language and was quite important. So mother taught both German and music. And I started playing piano at a very early age. But I was lazy. [LAUGHS] And we use to get struck with a ruler over my knuckles. But it didn't help much. I never practiced properly. So, that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anybody else live with you after your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: After my father died, my mother's mother, my grandmother, moved in with us. And she looked after me, while my mother was working. And she's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get on with your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I loved her very dearly. But she always complained that I was cheeky. [LAUGHTER] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have-- so you had no brothers or sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no,no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were the only one.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was the only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any cousins living nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two cousins I was very close to, who lived about 30K away. And I'm still very close to them now. They were practically my extended family. My male cousin was about four months older than I. And we practically grew up together. And when I went there for the weekend, I was allowed to do all the things I wasn't allowed to do at home, like getting dirty, and all that. So I loved going there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do they live now?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a big-- they had a big garden. And since then, I love gardening.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so that was the start of your love of gardening.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we do they live now?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- well, my male cousin still lives in the paternal home in Brandys, which is just a bit north of Prague. And my cousin married a Frenchman and lives in France, in-- near Paris. So I've got three-- she's got three children. I'm still very close to them. And I'm also close to my cousin's children in Czechoslovakia. And my mother had a brother who was, who went to Palestine after the war. And then unfortunately, he had very severe malaria, and the doctors advised him to go back to Europe, to cure himself. And he got caught. He couldn't get out anymore. And so he managed to send the two boys to England, with the children's transports. But he and his wife perished in the last transport. They went straight to the gas in October, '44. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was never very close to those two. Because they used to always call and fight and-- [LAUGHS] they were two boys, and they always fought. So I, I never liked to be together with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the names of the cousins you were close to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first names or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Their first names, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zdenek, which is Sydney, and Marie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And in your family in Czechoslovakia, were you religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were, we were-- my grandmothers were still religious. But my parents' generation not. I mean, we-- my mother still fasted when she was 89 and all that. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So she did observe--

speaker: interviewee
text: She observed. But we were not kosher or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Did she go to synagogue on the Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always took my grandmother to the synagogue on, on the High Holidays. I always went with her. And of course, I went to-- had religious instruction. And later on, I learned Hebrew and all that. But we were not particularly observant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And did your family observe the Passover and those festivities?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go-- because we were just the three women--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --we used to be invited to some distant relations for Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did observe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't worry much--

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you think of yourself as Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So that was part of your identity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were most of your friends Jewish or Czech, or both?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Actually Czech--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean your family friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I grew up in an area which didn't have very many Jews. And I only became consciously Jewish after Hitler came, and we were not allowed to mix with the non-Jewish population. And I went to Jewish school then. Or rather, I took exams in the Jewish school. And I had a lot of-- we went-- there was a hockey ball, which was a sports area, where the Jews were permitted to go to. And I made a lot of Jewish friends there. But apart  of that, I didn't have very many Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alright. And what the very first school you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a-- it was called a progressive school, which was an experimental school, actually. We were given a lot of freedom to learn at our own pace. We were not taught by rote. We were not taught letters. We were taught whole words to learn. And we were encouraged to express ourselves. And I think it was a wonderful school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was quite unusual for those days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very unusual, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And different from the typical Germanic type of education.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, it was terrific, because we worked in groups. And each group progressed at their own pace. We corrected our own work. You, you had-- we didn't have books, we had sheets. We wrote on sheets. And when you finished your exercise, you went to the teacher and got the book of, of results. And then you compared your work to the results and said you had 90% or 95% or whatever. You marked it yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fantastic. So it's very empowering, that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it taught you responsibility and discipline, and I think honesty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yeah. [INTERPOSING VOICES] How interesting. And do you think that affected your intellectual development?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think all my life, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All your life. It must have given  you a lot of intellectual independence.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. And we did a lot of extracurricular things. And it, it was a terrific school, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose writings was that based on, that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it quite unusual for those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had a lot of visitors from overseas. And seeing that I spoke English-- or rather I learned English there, and I spoke German fluently. So I usually had to welcome the visiting teachers from overseas, which was-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] I quite enjoyed doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what got you to learn English?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother. My mother saw what was coming, with Hitler--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --gaining power in Germany. And she thought we might have to emigrate. And she spoke English, or she had learned English during her education. And so she had me taught English. And first, she engaged a refugee professor from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she realized I had a dreadful, guttural German accent in my English, so she promptly sent me to a lady from the British Institute, who knew no Czech. She was a genuine English lady. And so we use to read. She'd just-- we just read, and she'd correct my pronunciation. That was very interesting. Because once we came across the word 'virgin.' And I didn't quite know what it was. I was about eight or nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- so she said, it's a young woman. I was, what is it? And sort of she indicated for a young women. I said, oh, like you. Oh, she says, I'm not a virgin. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was puzzling about this for years. [LAUGHTER] She was really offended by that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so then she went back to England. And I don't-- I think by that time, Hitler came. And all teaching was-- we weren't allowed to learn anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you at this progressive school, from what age to what age?

speaker: interviewee
text: From age seven till 11 I should-- six and a half to 11. And it was five years, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you make good friends in that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, I had very many good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Czech and Jewish, or mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechs. There was only one Jewish girl. And we were great friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any anti-Semitism at the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when-- at the beginning of the year, when we had to say our names and religion. And I was always tall, so I sat in the last row. And when I said, 'Jewish,' the whole class turned around. They knew it jolly well for years. But they still turned around and had a look.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is this. So I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --there were two Jewish girls in the, in the school. And then we got another girl, who was a refugee from Germany. And of course, she spoke German and not much Czech. And everybody hated her. Because the Czechs hated the Germans. So, that was a funny situation. We tried to protect her. But then finally they emigrated somewhere, I don't know where, I don't know what.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in those first years of school, there wasn't open anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, never. No, never. I was, I was sort of the prefect or whatever of the school. And together, my male partner was the nephew of the president of the republic, so [LAUGHS] ask what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: He's coming, so he might want--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And coming back to your family life at that stage, you said your mother was aware that you might have to emigrate at some stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, with the rise of the, of the Hitler Reich, did she realize that in Czechoslovakia, you were sort of very close to Germany, and it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on the cards that something will happen. We-- Czechoslovakia did have a pact with France and England and also a small pact with, I think, Yugoslavia and Romania. But it was doubtful whether anybody will do very much. And my mother started looking for ways of getting out. Bear in mind that my mother was a widow. We didn't have much money. The pension she had was very, very small. Because my father was only employed for about six years or something like that when he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a very precarious existence we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, we had no connections. I mean, if you are in business or something, like my husband's family. They had factories, and they had all sorts of things in England and in America, so it was easier for them. For us, it was very difficult. And my mother finally found herself a job as a domestic in London. And she had the permission to bring me along, too. But then, Grandmother. [SIGHS] So you can't leave your mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the end, we didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: She wanted to send me with my cousins. But I didn't come back from a summer camp when I was summoned to come, because I was having a great time. And so I didn't go to England, which was just as well. Because my mother would have never survived on her own.  She only survived, I think, because she had the willpower--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --To survive with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So how old were you when war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Hitler came in '39, on the 15th of March, '39. I was 12 then. And well, war broke out very quickly after that. And we were allowed to finish the school year. But after, I think-- the university already excluded Jews by then. And people suffered economically, because they-- the shops were confiscated. Our banks, bank accounts were frozen. People were thrown out of their jobs. Anybody who had any prominent job, people were thrown out. And it became very difficult for Jews. Also, there was a great influx of Jews from Germany, from Austria, also from the Sudeten, after the Sudeten were occupied. So there were a lot of Jews who were really on the bread line in Prague. And it was very, very difficult. There were-- after Hitler came, then after the war broke out, there were-- practically every week, there were new laws what we weren't allowed to do. We got smaller rations. We only got the staples and in smaller quantities. We were only allowed to show up in certain shops, after a certain time, when practically everything was sold out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the afternoon or later--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 5:30 or whatever. 5 o'clock in the afternoon. So it was rather tenuous. I must say, a lot of our neighbors were very decent. They'd bring us things after dark, maybe a fish or maybe a piece of meat or maybe four eggs. Because they didn't have much either. But they still could go to the country and bring-- if they had relations somewhere--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they could bring things in. Whereas we couldn't leave the city. There was a curfew at 8, and we were not allowed to leave the city. We were not allowed to use public transport. At first, only in the last carriage and later not at all. We were not allowed to enter parks, sit on a bench, and go-- of course, go to theaters, concerts, or anything like that. And people in Prague complained they can't get into the church on Sunday, because the bloody Jews listened to the music. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the only place they were allowed to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was the only place they could go to. And there was always beautiful--

speaker: interviewer
text: Organ music.

speaker: interviewee
text: --organ music.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jews went into the church.

speaker: interviewee
text: So instead of concerts, they used to go 10 o'clock in the morning to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were three or four churches that had good music. So the parishioners complained to the authorities they can't get to the church because of the Jews. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing. And how long did you continue going to school for, after war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] About a year. And then we had secret sort of-- they called it circles-- about six, seven Jewish children went to a private home. We had to always go piecemeal. You couldn't ever go together, because that-- it was forbidden to teach Jewish children. And people who were university students or perhaps professional teachers came and taught us various subjects. And so I did that for a year. And we sort of were allowed to take external exams at the Jewish school. And then after-- the next year, after the transport started, the whole thing was in a state of flux. Because people sort of were taken into transports all the time, and you couldn't maintain it properly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, people didn't have much money anymore. And so the whole thing just petered out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what grade did you go up to you in your primary school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I finished-- I think, I'm not sure whether I finished third or fourth-year high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought I finished fourth-year. But I couldn't find the examination results, so perhaps I only finished [LAUGHS] third-year. But I think, I think it was fourth-year. And I did a course in, in graphics. I always liked drawing, so I did a course in graphics and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sort of craft and various other things, which you thought might be useful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So this progressive school you went to-- how old were you when you started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six and a half, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went till 13 or--

speaker: interviewee
text: 11 And a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right. That's the primary school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went two years to high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a different high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, district high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no Jewish schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was only the elementary school. And you see, in-- in Czechoslovakia, there were two grades of high school-- the academic high school, the gymnasium, as they called it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the citizen school, which was a lower grade. And the children were expected to finish at 14 and go into the workforce or do, be apprenticed or something like that. So I went to the academic school, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Gymnasium--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gymnasium, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And so you were still attending that when war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you'd done like three years.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- no, two years of the gymnasium, and then one or two years of the external. But I'm not-- I think two years, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any anti-Semitism at that gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there mixed Jewish and Czechoslovakian?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were, again, very few Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was not a popular area. It was an area-- when my father and mother got married, it was a great housing shortage after the war. And this was an area of a new blocks or flats were built. And they bought a-- what we'd call a unit--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --today. But it was not a fashionable area, it was on the outskirts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not where the Jews normally lived. So there are very few Jews in the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at this high school that you went to-- so it wasn't in Czechoslovakia, it wasn't like in some of the other schools, where there was a lot of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my husband in Vienna encountered a lot of it. But I didn't. The Czechs basically are not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are not anti-Semitic, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They don't like the Germans. So if the Jews speak with a German accent, they didn't like them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, but for a Czech-speaking Jew, that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: But for the Czech-speaking Jew, there was very little problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So did-- did you feel insecure living in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did, at the-- before Hitler came, there was the protectorate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was very anti-Semitic already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was run by the fascists.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were already anti-Jewish measures. And various professional organizations already clamored for the Jews to be excluded-- the lawyers, the doctors, the shopkeepers. And they were already clamoring for the Jews to be excluded from the professions. And of course, the association of high school students-- of not high school, university students, wanted numerous clauses for the Jews, because the Jews were in competition for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like the Asians today in Australia, the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So jealous of them having those positions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jealous of their progress, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And in the school that you went to privately, did you keep up the same level of academic standards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I should think-- I should think so. I should think so, because everybody worked as much-- you weren't forced to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You worked because you wanted to work. And you wanted to learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So that must be very difficult for your mother, being a single parent in those days.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very difficult. But I think, [SIGHS] thinking back, I thought she must have had some-- my mother was very well-read. And she used to read magazines-- English magazines and German papers. She used to go to-- one used to go to a coffee shop and read.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was extremely well-informed. And I think she could see what was happening in Germany--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think she probably took our money out of the bank. Because-- I can't remember, well, we were never flush. But I can't remember that we would have been completely penniless--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --like some people were when the accounts were frozen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, so that was quite a far-sighted thing to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she never contemplated remarrying--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she didn't want to. She was afraid that then I would be pushed aside, or that maybe-- I don't know, what was-- also, she didn't have the opportunity. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --she only saw fathers and mothers, sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, so she didn't move in a social circle, where there were single men.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she-- Well, they were all married people. And there were the parents of my friends and the parents of her pupils and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, so she really had to be like a mother and a father to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, that's right. Yes, she-- she was wonderful, really. She never spared any money to give me some special education. I went to lessons-- gymnastic lesson and dance lessons and music lessons and this and that, English and whatever I wanted to learn. And I wanted to learn the graphic arts, so I was going there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever go on holidays with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go on holidays. We used to take-- seeing that she wasn't working all the school holidays--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --we used to take a room somewhere in a private home and sort of just have-- go out, perhaps, for lunch or for dinner and prepare our meals otherwise. So-- But it was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to walk a lot. My mother was a great walker. I was too. I always loved walking. I met my husband in a bushwalking club. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing, amazing. Well, we'll get on-- we'll get on to that a  little bit later. And with your mother-- and your grandmother lived with you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in the home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she'd go to Theresienstadt too, to stay with her sisters over the holidays. And we'd go somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far was Theresienstadt from where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 60K north of Prague, about two hours' train journey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Well, what we'll do is on the next tape, we'll discuss how your life changed when the war broke out, and what your experiences were then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.
